perturbation.original_text: Prices are exclusive of any tax, value-added tax, fee, duty or governmental charge, however designated (except for NETGEAR's franchise taxes or for taxes on NETGEAR's net income) which may be levied or based on the Products, their sale, importation, use, or possession, or on this Agreement. All such taxes or duties shall be for the account of Distributor and any such taxes or duties required to be paid or collected by NETGEAR shall be paid by Distributor to NETGEAR unless Distributor provides NETGEAR with a valid certificate of exemption acceptable to the appropriate taxing or governmental authority.
perturbation.changed_text: Prices are exclusive of any tax, value-added tax, fee, duty or governmental charge, however designated (except for NETGEAR's franchise taxes or for taxes on NETGEAR's net income) which may be levied or based on the Products, their sale, importation, use, or possession. All such taxes or duties shall be for the account of Distributor; however, NETGEAR, at its sole discretion, may choose to absorb some or all of these taxes as a promotional offer, without prior notice.
perturbation.explanation: Shifting the responsibility for taxes introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting tax regulations that mandate clear and consistent assignment of tax liabilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6051
perturbation.law_explanation: California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6051 requires a sales tax to be collected, making the responsibility of the Distributor to pay the taxes a set legal requirement. By including a clause that NETGEAR can 'absorb some or all of these taxes as a promotional offer, without prior notice,' there is no guarantee the taxes will be paid. This clause creates ambiguity regarding tax obligations, which could be construed as an attempt to evade legally mandated tax collection and payment, contradicting the requirements for clear tax liability as defined in CA Rev & Tax Code § 6051.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a contradiction by allowing NETGEAR to 'absorb some or all of these taxes as a promotional offer,' potentially undermining the legal requirement for the Distributor to pay all taxes as initially stated. The original text clearly places the responsibility for all taxes and duties on the Distributor. California Revenue and Taxation Code Section 6051, as shown in both scraped snippets, imposes a tax on retailers for the privilege of selling tangible personal property. By allowing NETGEAR to absorb these taxes, the modified text creates ambiguity about who is ultimately responsible for ensuring the tax is paid, potentially conflicting with the clear imposition of tax on the retailer as mandated by CA Rev & Tax Code § 6051.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor may not sell or license Products directly to end use customers without the express written consent of NETGEAR. Distributor agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to maximize sales of NETGEAR Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor agrees to use commercially reasonable efforts to maximize sales of NETGEAR Products. Distributor may not sell or license Products directly to end use customers without the express written consent of NETGEAR unless explicitly approved by the CEO in a verbal agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing the exception of 'verbal agreement with the CEO' to bypass written consent creates ambiguity and enforceability issues, potentially violating the Statute of Frauds regarding contracts that cannot be completed within one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1624 requires certain contracts, including those not performable within a year, to be in writing. The original clause mandates written consent for direct sales to end-users. The change introducing 'verbal agreement with the CEO' contradicts this by allowing a potentially long-term or high-value agreement to be based on oral consent, making it unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds as it falls under contracts that cannot be completed within one year and must be in writing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a lo
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a verbal agreement exception that undermines the Statute of Frauds. California Civil Code Section 1624(a)(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that agreements not to be performed within a year must be in writing. The altered contract allows potentially long-term agreements to be based on a verbal agreement with the CEO, thus creating an exception to the writing requirement for contracts exceeding one year. This is a structural flaw that leads to a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned by either party without prior written permission from the other party, which permission shall not be unreasonably withheld or delayed. Any attempt by either party to assign any right, or delegate any duty or obligation which arises under the Agreement without such permission will be voidable.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may not be assigned by either party without prior written permission from the other party. However, should a circumstance arise that one party should assign any right, duty or obligation to another party without notifying the other party, and that assignment has lasted for at least 6 months without a formal complaint of breach, the action will be accepted as valid by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the assignment clause to allow implied acceptance through a 6-month period contradicts the original requirement of prior written permission.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1541 states that an obligation is extinguished by a release therefrom given to the debtor by the creditor, upon a new consideration, or in writing, with or without new consideration. The original clause requires written permission for assignment. The added clause, permitting assignment to be accepted if it remains unchallenged for six months, conflicts with this, creating an implied release or waiver that isn't in writing, thereby contradicting the requirements for modifying contractual obligations.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 1. OBLIGATIONS IN GENERAL [1427 - 1543] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. EXTINCTION OF OBLIGATIONS [1473 - 1543] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Release [1541 - 1543] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1541. An obligation is extinguished by a release therefrom given to the debtor or the released party by the creditor or releasing party, upon a new consideration, or in writing, with or without new consideration. (Amended by Stats. 2018, Ch. 157, Sec. 1. (SB 1431) Effective January 1, 2019.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires prior written permission for assignment. The changed text allows assignment to be accepted if unchallenged for six months, conflicting with the written requirement. California Civil Code Section 1541, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies that an obligation is extinguished by a release given to the debtor in writing. Allowing an assignment to be accepted without written consent directly contradicts this requirement, as the release or waiver is not explicitly in writing, creating a legal contradiction. The modified clause undermines the original intent and the legal requirement for written permission.
